conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me first review the official eligibility rules, and then I'll ask you about your situation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit. Let me start with a few questions:

**Do you have any children under the age of 16 (or under 20 if they're in approved education or training)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have two children I'd like to ask about. There's Alex, who's 17, and Blake, who's 18. They both live with me here in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know about Alex (17) and Blake (18) who both live with you in Leeds.

Since both of your children are over 16, their eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether they're in approved education or training. 

**Is Alex (who is 17) currently in approved education or training?** For example, this could include A-levels, T-levels, NVQs up to level 3, or similar approved courses.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in sixth form college studying for their A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Since Alex is 17 and in sixth form studying A-levels, that counts as approved education.

Now, regarding Blake who is 18:

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake finished school and isn't doing any courses or training at the moment. They're just taking some time to figure out what they want to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask a couple more questions to complete my assessment.

**Has Blake recently left education or training (within the last 20 weeks), and if so, have they registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake left school a little while back now and hasn't registered with any careers service or the armed forces or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have a few final questions to make sure I have all the information I need:

**Are you responsible for both Alex and Blake - meaning you live with them and provide for their care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. They both live at home with me and I look after them both.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just one more question to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex and Blake.

Let me give you my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

**For Alex (age 17): ✓ You ARE eligible**
Since Alex is under 20 and is currently studying A-levels at sixth form college (which counts as approved education), you qualify for Child Benefit for Alex. As they live with you and you're responsible for them, and no one else is claiming, you can receive Child Benefit for Alex.

**For Blake (age 18): ✗ You are NOT eligible**
Unfortunately, Blake does not qualify for Child Benefit. Since Blake is over 16 and not currently in approved education or training, the eligibility criteria are not met. Child Benefit for children aged 16-19 only continues if they remain in approved education or training.

**What this means:**
You can claim or continue to receive Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Blake. If Blake decides to return to education or start approved training in the future (and is still under 20), you may become eligible to claim Child Benefit for them at that time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit entitlement?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is currently studying A-levels at sixth form college. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16, but you'll still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is under 20, lives with you, is in approved education (A-levels count as approved education), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 18 years old and has finished school. They are not currently in any approved education or training. Blake left school some time ago (more than 20 weeks) and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. According to the eligibility rules, you can only get Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or over if they are under 20 AND in approved education or training. There is also a provision for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a careers service or armed services, but Blake is 18 and has not done this, and it has been longer than 20 weeks. Since Blake does not meet the requirement of being in approved education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for two children, Alex (17) and Blake (18), both living with them in Leeds. Alex is eligible because they are under 20 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form). Blake is not eligible because they are over 16, not in approved education or training, and do not meet any of the exceptions that would allow Child Benefit to continue after leaving education.

Blake is 18 years old and has finished school. They are not currently in any approved education or training. Blake left school some time ago (more than 20 weeks) and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. According to the eligibility rules, you can only get Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or over if they are under 20 AND in approved education or training. There is also a provision for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education and registers with a careers service or armed services, but Blake is 18 and has not done this, and it has been longer than 20 weeks. Since Blake does not meet the requirement of being in approved education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Alex is eligible because they are under 20 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form). Blake is not eligible because they are over 16, not in approved education or training, and do not meet any of the exceptions that would allow Child Benefit to continue after leaving education.
